question.text: Which strategy helps a retiree avoid the risk of investing all retirement funds when interest rates are low?

answer.text: Investing the entire corpus in equity for higher returns
Staggering investment into income-generating products over time
Keeping all funds in a savings bank account
Borrowing money to invest when rates are expected to rise

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Staggering investments into income products (annuities, FDs, bonds) over time reduces the risk of locking in at a single point when rates may be unfavorable. This is similar to rupee cost averaging for income products. **The Trap:** Investing everything in equity (Option A) adds market risk. Savings accounts (Option C) earn very low returns. Borrowing (Option D) creates new obligations. **Cross-Exam Context:** Staggered investing for income generation is covered in Series XVII. Similar to SIP for accumulation, this strategy addresses reinvestment risk in the distribution stage. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When a client retires during a low-interest-rate period, don't rush to buy an annuity. Stagger across 2-3 years to capture different rate environments.
